rows.pali: Sasaṅkhāratā pana aniyatesu ca thinamiddhasambhavova viseso.
rows.english: the only difference, however, being promptedness and the presence of stiffness and torpor (xliii) among the inconstant.

rows.pali: 489.Mohamūlesu dvīsu vicikicchāsampayuttena tāva phasso, cetanā, vitakko, vicāro, vīriyaṃ, jīvitaṃ, cittaṭṭhiti, ahirikaṃ, anottappaṃ, moho, vicikicchāti sarūpena āgatā ekādasa (dha. sa. 422; dha. sa. aṭṭha. 422), uddhaccaṃ, manasikāroti yevāpanakā dve cāti terasa.
rows.english: 176. (32)–(33) As regards the two rooted in delusion, firstly: [associated] with [the consciousness that is] associated with uncertainty (32) [471] are the eleven given in the texts as such thus: contact (i), volition (ii), applied thought (iii), sustained thought (iv), energy (vi), life (vii), (xlix) steadiness of consciousness, consciencelessness (xxxvii), shamelessness (xxxviii), delusion (xl), (l) uncertainty. The or-whatever-states are these two: agitation (xlii), attention (xxx). And these together total thirteen.

rows.pali: 490.Tattha cittaṭṭhitīti pavattiṭṭhitimatto dubbalo samādhi.
rows.english: 177. Herein, (xlix) steadiness of consciousness is weak concentration (viii) consisting in mere steadiness in occurrence.70

rows.pali: Vigatā cikicchāti vicikicchā.
rows.english: (1) It is without wish to cure (vigatā cikicchā), thus it is uncertainty (vicikicchā).

rows.pali: Sā saṃsayalakkhaṇā, kampanarasā, anicchayapaccupaṭṭhānā, anekaṃsagāhapaccupaṭṭhānā vā, vicikicchāyaṃ ayonisomanasikārapadaṭṭhānā, paṭipattiantarāyakarāti daṭṭhabbā.
rows.english: It has the characteristic of doubt. Its function is to waver. It is manifested as indecisiveness, or it is manifested as taking various sides. Its proximate cause is unwise attention. It should be regarded as obstructive of theory (see XVII.52).

rows.pali: Sesā vuttappakārāyeva.
rows.english: The rest are as already described.

rows.pali: Uddhaccasampayuttena vicikicchāsampayutte vuttesu ṭhapetvā vicikicchaṃ sesā dvādasa.
rows.english: 178. (33) [The consciousness] associated with agitation (33) has the same [formations as the consciousness] associated with uncertainty (32), except for uncertainty (1).

rows.pali: Vicikicchāya abhāvena panettha adhimokkho uppajjati.
rows.english: But with the absence of uncertainty resolution (xxix) arises here.

rows.pali: Tena saddhiṃ teraseva, adhimokkhasabbhāvato ca balavataro samādhi hoti.
rows.english: So with that they are likewise thirteen, and concentration (viii) is stronger because of the presence of resolution.

rows.pali: Yañcettha uddhaccaṃ, taṃ sarūpeneva āgataṃ.
rows.english: Also agitation is given in the texts as such,

rows.pali: Adhimokkhamanasikārā yevāpanakavasenāti evaṃ akusalasaṅkhārā veditabbā.
rows.english: while resolution (xxix) and attention (xxx) are among the or-whatever-states. Thus should the unprofitable formations be understood.

rows.pali: 491.Abyākatesu vipākābyākatā tāva ahetukasahetukabhedato duvidhā.
rows.english: 179.III. As regards the indeterminate, firstly, the resultant indeterminate (34)–(69) are twofold, classed as those without root-cause and those with root-cause.

rows.pali: Tesu ahetukavipākaviññāṇasampayuttā ahetukā.
rows.english: Those associated with resultant consciousness without root-cause (34)–(41), (50)–(56) are those without root-cause.

rows.pali: Tattha kusalākusalavipākacakkhuviññāṇasampayuttā tāva phasso, cetanā, jīvitaṃ, cittaṭṭhitīti sarūpena āgatā cattāro (dha. sa. 431; dha. sa. aṭṭha. 431), yevāpanako manasikāroyevāti pañca.
rows.english: Herein, firstly, those associated with the profitable resultant (34) and unprofitable resultant (50) eye-consciousness are the four given in the texts as such, namely: contact (i), volition (ii), life (vii), steadiness of consciousness (xlix), which amount to five with attention (xxx) as the only or-whatever-state.

rows.pali: Sotaghānajivhākāyaviññāṇasampayuttāpi eteyeva.
rows.english: These same kinds are associated with ear-, nose-, tongue-, and body- consciousness (35)–(38), (51)–(54).

rows.pali: Ubhayavipākamanodhātuyā ete ceva vitakkavicārādhimokkhā cāti aṭṭha, tathā tividhāyapi ahetukamanoviññāṇadhātuyā.
rows.english: 180. Those associated with both kinds of resultant mind-element (39), (55) come to eight by adding applied thought (iii), sustained thought (iv) and resolution (xxix). Likewise those associated with the threefold mind- consciousness-element with root-cause (40), (41), (56).

rows.pali: Yā panettha somanassasahagatā, tāya saddhiṃ pīti adhikā hotīti veditabbā.
rows.english: But here (40) that accompanied by joy should be understood to have happiness (v) also in addition to that.

rows.pali: Sahetukavipākaviññāṇasampayuttā pana sahetukā.
rows.english: 181. The [formations] associated with resultant consciousness with root-cause (42)–(49) are those with root-cause.

rows.pali: Tesu aṭṭhakāmāvacaravipākasampayuttā tāva aṭṭhahi kāmāvacarakusalehi sampayuttasaṅkhārasadisāyeva.
rows.english: Of these, firstly, those associated with the sense-sphere resultant [consciousness] with root-cause are similar to the formations associated with the eight sense-sphere [consciousnesses] (1)–(8).

rows.pali: Yā pana tā aniyatesu karuṇāmuditā, tā sattārammaṇattā vipākesu na santi.
rows.english: But of the inconstant ones, compassion (xxxii) and gladness (xxxiii) are not among the resultant because they have living beings as their object.

rows.pali: Ekantaparittārammaṇā hi kāmāvacaravipākā.
rows.english: For the resultant ones of the sense-sphere have only limited objects.

rows.pali: Na kevalañca karuṇāmuditā, viratiyopi vipākesu na santi.
rows.english: And not only compassion and gladness but also the three abstinences (xxxiv)–(xxxvi) are not among the resultant; [472]

rows.pali: "Pañca sikkhāpadā kusalāyevā"ti hi vuttaṃ.
rows.english: for it is said that “the five training precepts are profitable only” (Vibh 291).

rows.pali: Rūpāvacarārūpāvacaralokuttaravipākaviññāṇasampayuttā pana tesaṃ kusalaviññāṇasampayuttasaṅkhārehi sadisā eva.
rows.english: 182. (57)–(69) Those associated with the resultant consciousness of the fine- material sphere (57)–(61), the immaterial sphere (62)–(65), and the supramundane (66)–(69) are similar to the formations associated with the profitable consciousnesses of those kinds (9)–(21) too.

rows.pali: 492.Kiriyābyākatāpi ahetukasahetukabhedato duvidhā.
rows.english: 183. (70)–(89) Functional indeterminate [formations] are also twofold classed as those without root-cause (70)–(72) and those with root-cause (73)–(80).

rows.pali: Tesu ahetukakiriyaviññāṇasampayuttā ahetukā.
rows.english: Those without root-cause are associated with functional consciousness without root- cause;

rows.pali: Te ca kusalavipākamanodhātuahetukamanoviññāṇadhātudvayayuttehi samānā.
rows.english: and they are the same as those associated [respectively] with profitable resultant mind-element (39) and the pair of mind-consciousness-elements without root-cause (40)–(41).

rows.pali: Manoviññāṇadhātudvaye pana vīriyaṃ adhikaṃ.
rows.english: But in the case of the two mind-consciousness- elements (71)–(72), energy (vi) is additional,

rows.pali: Vīriyasabbhāvato balappatto samādhi hoti.
rows.english: and because of the presence of energy, concentration (viii) is strong.

rows.pali: Ayamettha viseso.
rows.english: This is the difference here.

rows.pali: Sahetukakiriyaviññāṇasampayuttā pana sahetukā.
rows.english: 184. Those associated with functional consciousness with root-cause (73)– (80) are those with root-cause.

rows.pali: Tesu aṭṭhakāmāvacarakiriyaviññāṇasampayuttā tāva ṭhapetvā viratiyo aṭṭhahi kāmāvacarakusalehi sampayuttasaṅkhārasadisā.
rows.english: Of these, firstly, those associated with the eight sense-sphere functional consciousnesses (73)–(80) are similar to the formations associated with the eight sense-sphere profitable (1)–(8), except for the abstinences (xxxiv)–(xxxvi).

rows.pali: Rūpāvacarārūpāvacarakiriyasampayuttā pana sabbākārenapi tesaṃ kusalaviññāṇasampayuttasadisāyevāti evaṃ abyākatāpi saṅkhārā veditabbāti.
rows.english: Those associated with the functional [consciousnesses] of the fine-material sphere (81)–(85) and the immaterial sphere (86)–(89) are in all aspects similar to those associated with profitable consciousness (9)–(17). This is how formations should be understood as indeterminate.

rows.pali: Idaṃ tāva abhidhamme padabhājanīyanayena khandhesu vitthārakathāmukhaṃ.
rows.english: 185. The foregoing section, firstly, is that of the detailed explanation of the aggregates according to the Abhidhamma-Bhājaniya [of the Vibhaṅga].

rows.pali: 493.Bhagavatā pana –
rows.english: But by the Blessed One [in the Suttanta- Bhājaniya] in this way:

rows.pali: Evaṃ khandhā vitthāritā.
rows.english: - the aggregates have been given in detail

rows.pali: Tattha yaṃkiñcīti anavasesapariyādānaṃ.
rows.english: [MATERIALITY] 186. Herein, the word whatever includes without exception.

rows.pali: Rūpanti atippasaṅganiyamanaṃ.
rows.english: Materiality prevents over-generalization.

rows.pali: Evaṃ padadvayenāpi rūpassa anavasesapariggaho kato hoti.
rows.english: Thus materiality is comprised without exception by the two expressions.

rows.pali: Athassa atītādinā vibhāgaṃ ārabhati.
rows.english: Then he undertakes its exposition as past, future and present, etc.;

rows.pali: Tañhi kiñci atītaṃ, kiñci anāgatādibhedanti.
rows.english: for some of it is classed as past and some as future, and so on.

rows.pali: Esa nayo vedanādīsu.
rows.english: So also in the case of feeling, and so on.

rows.pali: 494.Tattha rūpaṃ tāva addhāsantatisamayakhaṇavasena catudhā atītaṃ nāma hoti.
rows.english: Herein, the materiality called (i) past is fourfold, according to (a) extent, (b) continuity, (c) period, and (d) moment.

rows.pali: Tathā anāgatapaccuppannaṃ.
rows.english: Likewise (ii) the future and (iii) the present. 71

rows.pali: Tattha addhāvasena tāva ekassa ekasmiṃ bhave paṭisandhito pubbe atītaṃ, cutito uddhaṃ anāgataṃ, ubhinnamantare paccuppannaṃ.
rows.english: 187. Herein, (a) firstly, according to extent: in the case of a single becoming of one [living being], previous to rebirth-linking is past, subsequent to death is future, between these two is present.

rows.pali: Santativasena sabhāgaekautusamuṭṭhānaṃ ekāhārasamuṭṭhānañca pubbāpariyavasena vattamānampi paccuppannaṃ, tato pubbe visabhāgautuāhārasamuṭṭhānaṃ atītaṃ, pacchā anāgataṃ.
rows.english: 188. (b) According to continuity: that [materiality] which has like or single origination72 by temperature and single origination by nutriment, though it occurs successively, [473] is present. That which, previous to that, was of unlike origination by temperature and nutriment is past. That which is subsequent is future.

rows.pali: Cittajaṃ ekavīthiekajavanaekasamāpattisamuṭṭhānaṃ paccuppannaṃ, tato pubbe atītaṃ, pacchā anāgataṃ.
rows.english: That which is born of consciousness and has its origination in one cognitive series, in one impulsion, in one attainment, is present. Previous to that is past. Subsequent to that is future.

rows.pali: Kammasamuṭṭhānassa pāṭiyekkaṃ santativasena atītādibhedo natthi, tesaññeva pana utuāhāracittasamuṭṭhānānaṃ upatthambhakavasena tassa atītādibhāvo veditabbo.
rows.english: There is no special classification into past continuity, etc., of that which has its origination in kamma, but its pastness, etc., should be understood according as it supports those which have their origination through temperature, nutriment, and consciousness.

rows.pali: Samayavasenaekamuhuttapubbaṇhasāyanharattindivādīsu samayesu santānavasena pavattamānaṃ taṃ taṃ samayaṃ paccuppannaṃ nāma, tato pubbe atītaṃ, pacchā anāgataṃ.
rows.english: 189. (c) According to period: any period among those such as one minute, morning, evening, day-and-night, etc., that occurs as a continuity, is called present. Previous to that is past. Subsequent is future.

rows.pali: Khaṇavasena uppādādikhaṇattayapariyāpannaṃ paccuppannaṃ, tato pubbe anāgataṃ, pacchā atītaṃ.
rows.english: 190.(d) According to moment: what is included in the trio of moments, [that is to say, arising, presence, and dissolution] beginning with arising is called present. At a time previous to that it is future. At a time subsequent to that it is past. 73

rows.pali: Apica atikkantahetupaccayakiccaṃ atītaṃ, niṭṭhitahetukiccaṃ aniṭṭhitapaccayakiccaṃ paccuppannaṃ, ubhayakiccaṃ asampattaṃ anāgataṃ.
rows.english: 191. Furthermore, that whose functions of cause and condition74 have elapsed is past. That whose function of cause is finished and whose function of condition is unfinished is present. That which has not attained to either function is future.

rows.pali: Sakiccakkhaṇe vā paccuppannaṃ, tato pubbe anāgataṃ, pacchā atītaṃ.
rows.english: Or alternatively, the moment of the function is present. At a time previous to that it is future. At a time subsequent to that it is past.

rows.pali: Ettha ca khaṇādikathāva nippariyāyā.
rows.english: And here only the explanations beginning with the moment are absolutely literal.

rows.pali: Sesā sapariyāyā.
rows.english: The rest are in a figurative [or relative] sense.

rows.pali: 495.Ajjhattabahiddhābhedo vuttanayo eva.
rows.english: 192. (iv)–(v) The division into internal and external is as already stated (§73).

rows.pali: Apica idha niyakajjhattampi ajjhattaṃ parapuggalikampi ca bahiddhāti veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: Besides, it is internal in the sense of one’s own75 that should be understood here as internal and that of another person as external.

rows.pali: Oḷārikasukhumabhedo vuttanayova.
rows.english: (vi)–(vii) Gross and subtle are also as already stated (§73).

rows.pali: 496.Hīnapaṇītabhedo duvidho pariyāyato nippariyāyato ca.
rows.english: 193. (viii)–(ix) Inferior and superior are twofold, namely, figuratively (relatively) and absolutely (literally).

rows.pali: Tattha akaniṭṭhānaṃ rūpato sudassīnaṃ rūpaṃ hīnaṃ.
rows.english: Herein, the materiality of the Sudassin deities is inferior to the materiality of the Akaniṭṭha (Highest) deities.

rows.pali: Tadeva sudassānaṃ rūpato paṇītaṃ.
rows.english: That same materiality [of the Sudassin deities] is superior to the materiality of the Sudassa deities.

rows.pali: Evaṃ yāva narakasattānaṃ rūpaṃ, tāva pariyāyato hīnapaṇītatā veditabbā.
rows.english: Thus, firstly, should inferiority and superiority be understood figuratively (relatively) down as far as the denizens of hell.

rows.pali: Nippariyāyato pana yattha akusalavipākaṃ uppajjati, taṃ hīnaṃ.
rows.english: But absolutely (literally) it is inferior where it arises as unprofitable result,

rows.pali: Yattha kusalavipākaṃ, taṃ paṇītaṃ.
rows.english: and it is superior where it arises as profitable result.76

rows.pali: Dūre santiketi idampi vuttanayameva.
rows.english: 194. (x)–(xi) Far and near: this is also as already described (§73).

rows.pali: Apica okāsatopettha upādāyupādāya dūrasantikatā veditabbā.
rows.english: Besides, relative farness and nearness should be understood here according to location.

rows.pali: 497.Tadekajjhaṃ abhisaṃyūhitvā abhisaṅkhipitvāti taṃ atītādīhi padehi visuṃ visuṃ niddiṭṭhaṃ rūpaṃ sabbaṃ ruppanalakkhaṇasaṅkhāte ekavidhabhāve paññāya rāsiṃ katvā rūpakkhandhoti vuccatīti ayamettha attho.
rows.english: 195. All that together in the mass and in the gross: by making all that materiality, separately described by the words “past,” etc., into a collection by understanding its oneness, in other words, its characteristic of being molested (ruppana), it comes to be called the materiality (rūpa) aggregate. This is the meaning here.

rows.pali: Etena sabbampi rūpaṃ ruppanalakkhaṇe rāsibhāvūpagamanena rūpakkhandhoti dassitaṃ hoti.
rows.english: 196. By this, too, it is shown that the materiality aggregate is all materiality, which all comes into the collection with the characteristic of being molested;

rows.pali: Na hi rūpato añño rūpakkhandho nāma atthi.
rows.english: for there is no materiality aggregate apart from materiality.

rows.pali: 498.Yathā ca rūpaṃ, evaṃ vedanādayopi vedayitalakkhaṇādīsu rāsibhāvūpagamanena.
rows.english: And just as in the case of materiality, so also feeling, etc., [are respectively shown as the feeling aggregate, etc.,] since they come under the collections with the [respective] characteristics of being felt, etc.;

rows.pali: Na hi vedanādīhi aññe vedanākkhandhādayo nāma atthi.
rows.english: for there is no feeling aggregate apart from feeling and so on.

rows.pali: Atītādivibhāge panettha santativasena khaṇādivasena ca vedanāya atītānāgatapaccuppannabhāvo veditabbo.
rows.english: [FEELING] 197. In the classification (i)–(iii) into past, etc., the past, future, and present state of feeling should be understood according to continuity and according to moment and so on.

rows.pali: Tattha santativasena ekavīthiekajavanaekasamāpattipariyāpannā ekavīthivisayasamāyogappavattā ca paccuppannā, tato pubbe atītā, pacchā anāgatā.
rows.english: Herein, according to continuity, that included in a single cognitive series, a single impulsion, a single attainment, and that occurring in association with an objective field of one kind,77 is present. Before that is past. Subsequent is future.

rows.pali: Khaṇādivasena khaṇattayapariyāpannā pubbantāparantamajjhattagatā sakiccañca kurumānā vedanā paccuppannā, tato pubbe atītā, pacchā anāgatā.
rows.english: According to moment, etc.: that feeling included in the trio of moments, which is in between the past time and the future time, and which is performing its own function, is present. Before that is past. Subsequent is future.

rows.pali: Ajjhattabahiddhābhedo niyakajjhattavasena veditabbo.
rows.english: 198.(iv)–(v)The classification into internal and external should be understood according to the internal in the sense of one’s own.

rows.pali: 499.Oḷārikasukhumabhedo "akusalā vedanā oḷārikā, kusalābyākatā vedanā sukhumā"tiādinā (vibha. 11) nayena vibhaṅge vuttena jātisabhāvapuggalalokiyalokuttaravasena veditabbo.
rows.english: (vi)–(vii) The classification into gross and subtle should be understood (a) according to kind, (b) individual essence, (c) person, and (d) the mundane and supramundane, as stated in the Vibhaṅga in the way beginning “Unprofitable feeling is gross, profitable and indeterminate feeling is subtle, [profitable and unprofitable feeling is gross, indeterminate feeling is subtle]” (Vibh 3), and so on.

rows.pali: Jātivasena tāva akusalā vedanā sāvajjakiriyahetuto, kilesasantāpabhāvato ca avūpasantavuttīti kusalavedanāya oḷārikā, sabyāpārato, saussāhato, savipākato, kilesasantāpabhāvato, sāvajjato ca vipākābyākatāya oḷārikā, savipākato, kilesasantāpabhāvato, sabyābajjhato, sāvajjato ca kiriyābyākatāya oḷārikā.
rows.english: 199. (a) According to kind, firstly: unprofitable feeling is a state of disquiet, because it is the cause of reprehensible actions and because it produces burning of defilement, so it is gross [compared] with profitable feeling. And because it is accompanied by interestedness and drive and result, and because of the burning of the defilements, and because it is reprehensible, it is gross compared with resultant indeterminate. Also because it is accompanied by result, because of the burning of the defilements, and because it is attended by affliction and is reprehensible, it is gross compared with functional indeterminate.

rows.pali: Kusalābyākatā pana vuttavipariyāyato akusalāya sukhumā.
rows.english: But in the opposite sense profitable and indeterminate feeling are subtle compared with unprofitable feeling.

rows.pali: Dvepi kusalākusalavedanā sabyāpārato, saussāhato, savipākato ca yathāyogaṃ duvidhāyapi abyākatāya oḷārikā, vuttavipariyāyena duvidhāpi abyākatā tāhi sukhumā.
rows.english: Also the two, that is, profitable and unprofitable feeling, involve interestedness, drive and result, so they are respectively gross compared with the twofold indeterminate. And in the opposite sense the twofold indeterminate is subtle compared with them.

rows.pali: Evaṃ tāva jātivasena oḷārikasukhumatā veditabbā.
rows.english: This, firstly, is how grossness and subtlety should be understood according to kind.

rows.pali: 500.Sabhāvavasena pana dukkhā vedanā nirassādato, savipphārato, khobhakaraṇato, ubbejanīyato, abhibhavanato ca itarāhi dvīhi oḷārikā, itarā pana dve sātato, santato, paṇītato, manāpato, majjhattato ca yathāyogaṃ dukkhāya sukhumā.
rows.english: 200.(b) According to individual essence: painful feeling is gross compared with the others because it is without enjoyment, it involves intervention, causes disturbance, creates anxiety, and is overpowering. The other two are subtle compared with the painful because they are satisfying, peaceful, and superior, and respectively agreeable and neutral.

rows.pali: Ubho pana sukhadukkhā savipphārato, khobhakaraṇato, pākaṭato ca adukkhamasukhāya oḷārikā, sā vuttavipariyāyena tadubhayato sukhumā.
rows.english: Both the pleasant and the painful are gross compared with the neither-painful-nor-pleasant because they involve intervention, cause disturbance and are obvious. The latter is subtle in the way aforesaid compared with both the former.

rows.pali: Evaṃ sabhāvavasena oḷārikasukhumatā veditabbā.
rows.english: Thus should grossness and subtlety be understood according to individual essence.

rows.pali: 501.Puggalavasena pana asamāpannassa vedanā nānārammaṇe vikkhittabhāvato samāpannassa vedanāya oḷārikā, vipariyāyena itarā sukhumā.
rows.english: 201. (c) According to person: feeling in one who has no attainment is gross compared with that in one who has one, because it is distracted by a multiple object. In the opposite sense the other is subtle.

rows.pali: Evaṃ puggalavasena oḷārikasukhumatā veditabbā.
rows.english: This is how grossness and subtlety should be understood according to person. [475]

rows.pali: Lokiyalokuttaravasenapana sāsavā vedanā lokiyā, sā āsavuppattihetuto, oghaniyato, yoganiyato, ganthaniyato, nīvaraṇiyato, upādāniyato, saṃkilesikato, puthujjanasādhāraṇato ca anāsavāya oḷārikā.
rows.english: 202. (d) According to the mundane and supramundane: feeling subject to cankers is mundane, and that is gross compared with that free from cankers, because it is the cause for the arising of cankers, is liable to the floods, liable to the bonds, liable to the ties, liable to the hindrances, liable to the clingings, defilable, and shared by ordinary men.

rows.pali: Sā vipariyāyena sāsavāya sukhumā.
rows.english: The latter, in the opposite sense, is subtle compared with that subject to cankers.

rows.pali: Evaṃ lokiyalokuttaravasena oḷārikasukhumatā veditabbā.
rows.english: This is how grossness and subtlety should be understood according to the mundane and supramundane.

rows.pali: 502.Tattha jātiādivasena sambhedo pariharitabbo.
rows.english: 203. Herein, one should beware of mixing up [the classifications] according to kind and so on.

rows.pali: Akusalavipākakaāyaviññāṇasampayuttā hi vedanā jātivasena abyākatattā sukhumāpi samānā sabhāvādivasena oḷārikā hoti.
rows.english: For although feeling associated with unprofitable resultant body-consciousness is subtle according to kind because it is indeterminate, it is nevertheless gross according to individual essence, and so on.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ "abyākatā vedanā sukhumā.
rows.english: And this is said: “Indeterminate feeling is subtle,

rows.pali: Dukkhā vedanā oḷārikā.
rows.english: painful feeling is gross.

rows.pali: Samāpannassa vedanā sukhumā.
rows.english: The feeling in one with an attainment is subtle,

rows.pali: Asamāpannassa vedanā oḷārikā.
rows.english: that in one with no attainment is gross.

rows.pali: Sāsavā vedanā oḷārikā.
rows.english: Feeling free from cankers is subtle,

rows.pali: Anāsavā vedanā sukhumā"ti (vibha. 11).
rows.english: feeling accompanied by cankers is gross” (Vibh 3).

rows.pali: Yathā ca dukkhā vedanā, evaṃ sukhādayopi jātivasena oḷārikā sabhāvādivasena sukhumā honti.
rows.english: And like painful feeling, so also pleasant, etc., is gross according to kind and subtle according to individual essence.

rows.pali: Tasmā yathā jātiādivasena sambhedo na hoti, tathā vedanānaṃ oḷārikasukhumatā veditabbā.
rows.english: 204. Therefore feeling’s grossness and subtlety should be understood in such a way that there is no mixing up of the classifications according to kind and so on.

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ – abyākatā jātivasena kusalākusalāhi sukhumā.
rows.english: For instance, [when it is said] “The indeterminate according to kind is subtle compared with the profitable and the unprofitable,” the individual-essence class, etc.,

rows.pali: Tattha katamā abyākatā?
rows.english: “Which kind of indeterminate?

rows.pali: Kiṃ dukkhā?
rows.english: Is it the painful?

rows.pali: Kiṃ sukhā?
rows.english: Is it the pleasant?

rows.pali: Kiṃ samāpannassa?
rows.english: Is it that in one with an attainment?

rows.pali: Kiṃ asamāpannassa?
rows.english: Is it that in one with no attainment?

rows.pali: Kiṃ sāsavā ?
rows.english: Is it that subject to cankers?

rows.pali: Kiṃ anāsavāti?
rows.english: Is it that free from cankers?

rows.pali: Evaṃ sabhāvādibhedo na parāmasitabbo.
rows.english: - must not be insisted upon like this.

rows.pali: Esa nayo sabbattha.
rows.english: ” and so in each instance.

rows.pali: Apica taṃ taṃ vā pana vedanaṃ upādāyupādāya vedanā oḷārikasukhumā daṭṭhabbāti vacanato akusalādīsupi lobhasahagatāya dosasahagatā vedanā aggi viya attano nissayadahanato oḷārikā, lobhasahagatā sukhumā.
rows.english: 205. Furthermore, because of the words “Or feeling should be regarded as gross or subtle in comparison with this or that feeling” (Vibh 4), among the unprofitable, etc., feeling accompanied by hate, too, is gross compared with that accompanied by greed because it burns up its own support, like a fire; and that accompanied by greed is subtle.

rows.pali: Dosasahagatāpi niyatā oḷārikā, aniyatā sukhumā.
rows.english: Also, that accompanied by hate is gross when the hate is constant, and subtle when it is inconstant.

rows.pali: Niyatāpi kappaṭṭhitikā oḷārikā, itarā sukhumā.
rows.english: And the constant is gross when giving result that lasts for the aeon, while the other is subtle.

rows.pali: Kappaṭṭhitikāsupi asaṅkhārikā oḷārikā, itarā sukhumā.
rows.english: And of those giving result lasting for the aeon the unprompted is gross, while the other is subtle.

rows.pali: Lobhasahagatā pana diṭṭhisampayuttā oḷārikā, itarā sukhumā.
rows.english: But that accompanied by greed is gross when associated with [false] view, while the other is subtle.

rows.pali: Sāpi niyatā kappaṭṭhitikā asaṅkhārikā oḷārikā, itarā sukhumā.
rows.english: That also when constant and giving result lasting for the aeon and unprompted is gross, while the others are subtle.

rows.pali: Avisesena ca akusalā bahuvipākā oḷārikā, appavipākā sukhumā.
rows.english: And without distinction the unprofitable with much result is gross, while that with little result is subtle.

rows.pali: Kusalā pana appavipākā oḷārikā, bahuvipākā sukhumā.
rows.english: But the profitable with little result is gross, while that with much result is subtle.

rows.pali: Apica kāmāvacarakusalā oḷārikā.
rows.english: 206. Furthermore, the profitable of the sense sphere is gross;

rows.pali: Rūpāvacarā sukhumā.
rows.english: that of the fine- material sphere is subtle;

rows.pali: Tato arūpāvacarā.
rows.english: next to which the immaterial,

rows.pali: Tato lokuttarā.
rows.english: and next the supramundane [should be similarly compared].

rows.pali: Kāmāvacarā dānamayā oḷārikā.
rows.english: That of the sense sphere is gross in giving,

rows.pali: Sīlamayā sukhumā.
rows.english: while it is subtle in virtue;

rows.pali: Tato bhāvanāmayā.
rows.english: next, that in development.

rows.pali: Bhāvanāmayāpi duhetukā oḷārikā.
rows.english: Also, that in development is gross with two root-causes,

rows.pali: Tihetukā sukhumā.
rows.english: while with three root-causes it is subtle.

rows.pali: Tihetukāpi sasaṅkhārikā oḷārikā.
rows.english: Also that with three root-causes is gross when prompted,

rows.pali: Asaṅkhārikā sukhumā.
rows.english: while it is subtle when unprompted.

rows.pali: Rūpāvacarā ca paṭhamajjhānikā oḷārikā - pe - pañcamajjhānikā sukhumā.
rows.english: That of the fine-material sphere is gross in the first jhāna, [while it is subtle in the second jhāna. That also of the second jhāna is gross] … of the fifth jhāna is subtle.

rows.pali: Arūpāvacarā ca ākāsānañcāyatanasampayuttā oḷārikā - pe - nevasaññānāsaññāyatanasampayuttā sukhumāva.
rows.english: And that of the immaterial sphere associated with the base consisting of boundless space is gross … [476] that associated with the base consisting of neither-perception-nor-non-perception is subtle only.

rows.pali: Lokuttarā ca sotāpattimaggasampayuttā oḷārikā - pe - arahattamaggasampayuttā sukhumāva.
rows.english: And the supramundane associated with the stream-entry path is gross … that associated with the Arahant path is subtle only.

rows.pali: Esa nayo taṃ taṃ bhūmivipākakiriyavedanāsu ca dukkhādiasamāpannādisāsavādivasena vuttavedanāsu ca.
rows.english: The same method applies also to resultant and functional feeling in the various planes and to feeling stated according to pain, etc., according to one with no attainment, etc., and according to that subject to cankers, and so on.

rows.pali: Okāsavasena cāpi niraye dukkhā oḷārikā.
rows.english: 207. Then according to location, painful feelings in hell are gross,

rows.pali: Tiracchānayoniyaṃ sukhumā - pe - paranimmitavasavattīsu sukhumāva.
rows.english: while in the animal generation they are subtle … Those among the Paranimmitavasavatti Deities are subtle only.

rows.pali: Yathā ca dukkhā, evaṃ sukhāpi sabbattha yathānurūpaṃ yojetabbā.
rows.english: And the pleasant should be construed throughout like the painful where suitable.

rows.pali: Vatthuvasena cāpi hīnavatthukā yā kāci vedanā oḷārikā, paṇītavatthukā sukhumā.
rows.english: 208. And according to physical basis, any feeling that has an inferior physical basis is gross, while one with a superior physical basis is subtle.

rows.pali: Hīnapaṇītabhede yā oḷārikā, sā hīnā.
rows.english: (viii)–(ix) What is gross should be regarded as inferior in the inferior-superior classification,

rows.pali: Yā ca sukhumā, sā paṇītāti daṭṭhabbā.
rows.english: and what is subtle superior.

rows.pali: 503.Dūrapadaṃ pana "akusalā vedanā kusalābyākatāhi vedanāhi dūre".
rows.english: 209. [(x)–(xi) The word far is explained in the Vibhaṅga in the way beginning “The unprofitable is far from the profitable and indeterminate” (Vibh 4)

rows.pali: Santikepadaṃ "akusalā vedanā akusalāya vedanāya santike"tiādinā nayena vibhaṅge vibhattaṃ.
rows.english: and the word near in the way beginning “Unprofitable feeling is near to unprofitable feeling” (Vibh 4).

rows.pali: Tasmā akusalā vedanā visabhāgato, asaṃsaṭṭhato, asarikkhato ca kusalābyākatāhi dūre, tathā kusalābyākatā akusalāya.
rows.english: Therefore, unprofitable feeling is far from the profitable and the indeterminate because of dissimilarity, unconnectedness, and non- resemblance. The profitable and the indeterminate are likewise far from the unprofitable.

rows.pali: Esa nayo sabbavāresu.
rows.english: And so in all instances.

rows.pali: Akusalā pana vedanā sabhāgato, sarikkhato ca akusalāya santiketi.
rows.english: But unprofitable feeling is near to unprofitable feeling because of similarity and resemblance.

rows.pali: Idaṃ vedanākkhandhassa atītādivibhāge vitthārakathāmukhaṃ.
rows.english: This is the section of the detailed explanation dealing with the past, etc., classifications of the feeling aggregate.

rows.pali: Taṃtaṃvedanāsampayuttānaṃ pana saññādīnampi evameva veditabbaṃ.
rows.english: [PERCEPTION, FORMATIONS AND CONSCIOUSNESS] 210. This should also be understood of the perception, etc., associated with any kind of feeling.

rows.pali: 504.Evaṃ viditvā ca puna etesveva –
rows.english: Having understood this, again as regards these same aggregates:

rows.pali: Tattha kamatoti idha uppattikkamo, pahānakkamo, paṭipattikkamo, bhūmikkamo, desanākkamoti bahuvidho kamo.
rows.english: 211. 1. Herein, as to order: order is of several kinds, namely, order of arising, order of abandoning, order of practice, order of plane, order of teaching.

rows.pali: Tattha "paṭhamaṃ kalalaṃ hoti, kalalā hoti abbuda"nti (saṃ. ni. 1.235) evamādi uppattikkamo.
rows.english: Herein, “First there comes to be the foetus in the first stage, then there comes to be the foetus in the second stage” (S I 206), etc., is order of arising.

rows.pali: "Dassanena pahātabbā dhammā, bhāvanāya pahātabbā dhammā"ti (dha. sa. tikamātikā 8) evamādi pahānakkamo.
rows.english: “Things to be abandoned by seeing, things to be abandoned by development” (Dhs 1), etc., is order of abandoning.

rows.pali: "Sīlavisuddhi, cittavisuddhī"ti (ma. ni. 1.259; paṭi. ma. 3.41) evamādi paṭipattikkamo.
rows.english: “Purification of virtue … purification of consciousness” (M I 148), etc., is order of practice.

rows.pali: "Kāmāvacarā, rūpāvacarā"ti (dha. sa. 987) evamādi bhūmikkamo.
rows.english: “The sense sphere, the fine- material sphere” (Paṭis I 83), etc., is order of plane.

rows.pali: "Cattāro satipaṭṭhānā, cattāro sammappadhānā"ti (dī. ni. 3.145) vā, "dānakathaṃ, sīlakatha"nti (dī. ni. 1.298) vā evamādi desanākkamo.
rows.english: “The four foundations of mindfulness, the four right efforts” (D II 120), etc., or “Talk on giving, talk on virtue” (M I 379), etc., is order of teaching.

rows.pali: Tesu idha uppattikkamo tāva na yujjati, kalalādīnaṃ viya khandhānaṃ pubbāpariyavavatthānena anuppattito.
rows.english: 212. Of these, firstly, order of arising is not applicable here because the aggregates do not arise in the order in which they are successively dealt with, as is the case with “the foetus in the first stage,” etc.,

rows.pali: Na pahānakkamo, kusalābyākatānaṃ appahātabbato.
rows.english: nor is order of abandoning applicable, because the profitable and indeterminate are not to be abandoned;

rows.pali: Napaṭipattikkamo, akusalānaṃ appaṭipajjanīyato.
rows.english: nor is order of practice, because what is unprofitable is not to be practiced;

rows.pali: Na bhūmikkamo, vedanādīnaṃ catubhūmipariyāpannattā.
rows.english: nor is order of plane, because feeling, etc., are included in all four planes.

rows.pali: Desanākkamo pana yujjati.
rows.english: 213. Order of teaching is appropriate however;

rows.pali: Abhedena hi pañcasu khandhesu attagāhapatitaṃ veneyyajanaṃ samūhaghanavinibbhogadassanena attagāhato mocetukāmo bhagavā hitakāmo tassa tassa janassa sukhagahaṇatthaṃ cakkhuādīnampi visayabhūtaṃ oḷārikaṃ paṭhamaṃ rūpakkhandhaṃ desesi.
rows.english: for there are those people who, while teachable, have fallen into assuming a self among the five aggregates owing to failure to analyze them; and the Blessed One is desirous of releasing them from the assumption by getting them to see how the [seeming] compactness of mass [in the five aggregates] is resolved; and being desirous of their welfare, he first, for the purpose of their easy apprehension, taught the materiality aggregate, which is gross, being the objective field of the eye, etc.;

rows.pali: Tato iṭṭhāniṭṭharūpasaṃvedanikaṃ vedanaṃ.
rows.english: and after that, feeling, which feels matter as desirable and undesirable;

rows.pali: "Yaṃ vedayati, taṃ sañjānātī"ti evaṃ vedanāvisayassa ākāragāhikaṃ saññaṃ.
rows.english: then perception, which apprehends the aspects of feeling’s objective field, since “What one feels, that one perceives” (M I 293);

rows.pali: Saññāvasena abhisaṅkhārake saṅkhāre.
rows.english: then formations, which form volitionally through the means of perception;

rows.pali: Tesaṃ vedanādīnaṃ nissayaṃ adhipatibhūtañca nesaṃ viññāṇanti evaṃ tāva kamato vinicchayanayo viññātabbo.
rows.english: and lastly, consciousness, which these things beginning with feeling have as their support, and which dominates them.78 This, in the first place, is how the exposition should be known as to order.

rows.pali: 505.Visesatoti khandhānañca upādānakkhandhānañca visesato.
rows.english: 214.2. As to distinction: as to the distinction between aggregates and aggregates- as-objects-of-clinging.

rows.pali: Ko pana nesaṃ viseso, khandhā tāva avisesato vuttā.
rows.english: But what is the distinction between them? Firstly, aggregates is said without distinguishing.

rows.pali: Upādānakkhandhā sāsavaupādāniyabhāvena visesetvā.
rows.english: Aggregates [as objects] of clinging is said distinguishing those that are subject to cankers and are liable to the clingings,

rows.pali: Yathāha –
rows.english: according as it is said:

rows.pali: Ettha ca yathā vedanādayo anāsavāpi atthi, na evaṃ rūpaṃ.
rows.english: 215. Now, while there is feeling, etc., both free from cankers [and subject to them],79 not so materiality.

rows.pali: Yasmā panassa rāsaṭṭhena khandhabhāvo yujjati, tasmā khandhesu vuttaṃ.
rows.english: However, since materiality can be described as a [simple] aggregate in the sense of a total, it is therefore mentioned among the [simple] aggregates.

rows.pali: Yasmā rāsaṭṭhena ca sāsavaṭṭhena ca upādānakkhandhabhāvo yujjati, tasmā upādānakkhandhesu vuttaṃ.
rows.english: And since it can be described as an aggregate [that is the object] of clinging in the sense of a total and in the sense of being subjected to cankers, that [same materiality] is therefore mentioned among the aggregates [as objects] of clinging too.

rows.pali: Vedanādayo pana anāsavāva khandhesu vuttā.
rows.english: But feeling, etc., are only mentioned among the [simple] aggregates when they are free from cankers.

rows.pali: Sāsavā upādānakkhandhesu.
rows.english: When they are subject to cankers, they are mentioned among the aggregates [as objects] of clinging.

rows.pali: Upādānakkhandhāti cettha upādānagocarā khandhā upādānakkhandhāti evamattho daṭṭhabbo.
rows.english: And here the meaning of the term “aggregates as objects of clinging” should be regarded as this: aggregates that are the resort of clinging are aggregates of clinging.

rows.pali: Idha pana sabbepete ekajjhaṃ katvā khandhāti adhippetā.
rows.english: But here all these taken together are intended as aggregates.

rows.pali: 506.Anūnādhikatoti kasmā pana bhagavatā pañceva khandhā vuttā anūnā anadhikāti.
rows.english: 216. 3. As to neither less nor more: but why are five aggregates, neither less nor more, mentioned by the Blessed One?

rows.pali: Sabbasaṅkhatasabhāgekasaṅgahato attattaniyagāhavatthussa etaparamato aññesañca tadavarodhato.
rows.english: (a) Because all formed things that resemble each other fall into these groups, (b) because that is the widest limit as the basis for the assumption of self and what pertains to self, and (c) because of the inclusion80 by them of the other sorts of aggregates.

rows.pali: Anekappabhedesu hi saṅkhatadhammesu sabhāgavasena saṅgayhamānesu rūpampi rūpasabhāgekasaṅgahavasena eko khandho hoti.
rows.english: 217. (a) When the numerous categories of formed states are grouped together according to similarity,81 materiality forms one aggregate through being grouped together according to similarity consisting in materiality;

rows.pali: Vedanā vedanāsabhāgekasaṅgahavasena eko khandho hoti.
rows.english: feeling forms one aggregate through being grouped together according to similarity consisting in feeling;

rows.pali: Esa nayo saññādīsu.
rows.english: and so with perception and the other two.

rows.pali: Tasmā sabbasaṅkhatasabhāgekasaṅgahato pañceva vuttā.
rows.english: So they are stated as five because similar formed things fall into groups.

rows.pali: Etaparamañcetaṃ attattaniyagāhavatthu yadidaṃ rūpādayo pañca.
rows.english: 218. (b) And this is the extreme limit as the basis for the assumption of self and what pertains to self, that is to say, the five beginning with materiality.

rows.pali: Vuttañhetaṃ "rūpe kho, bhikkhave, sati rūpaṃ upādāya rūpaṃ abhinivissa evaṃ diṭṭhi uppajjati 'etaṃ mama, esohamasmi, eso me attā'ti.
rows.english: For this is said: “Bhikkhus, when matter exists, it is through clinging to matter, through insisting upon (interpreting) matter, that such a view as this arises: ‘This is mine, this is I, this is my self.’

rows.pali: Vedanāya, saññāya, saṅkhāresu, viññāṇe sati viññāṇaṃ upādāya viññāṇaṃ abhinivissa evaṃ diṭṭhi uppajjati 'etaṃ mama, esohamasmi, eso me attā"ti (saṃ. ni. 3.207).
rows.english: When feeling exists … When perception exists … When formations exist … When consciousness exists, it is through clinging to consciousness, through insisting upon (interpreting) consciousness, that such a view as this arises: ‘This is mine, this is I, this is my self’”(S III 181–82).

rows.pali: Tasmā attattaniyagāhavatthussa etaparamatopi pañceva vuttā.
rows.english: So they are stated as five because this is the widest limit as a basis for the assumption of self and what pertains to self.

rows.pali: Yepi caññe sīlādayo pañca dhammakkhandhā vuttā, tepi saṅkhārakkhandhe pariyāpannattā ettheva avarodhaṃ gacchanti.
rows.english: 219. (c) And also, since those other [sorts of aggregates] stated as the five aggregates of things beginning with virtue82 are comprised within the formations aggregate, they are included here too.

rows.pali: Tasmā aññesaṃ tadavarodhatopi pañceva vuttāti evaṃ anūnādhikato vinicchayanayo viññātabbo.
rows.english: Therefore they are stated as five because they include the other sorts. This is how the exposition should be known as to neither less nor more.

rows.pali: 507.Upamātoti ettha hi gilānasālupamo rūpupādānakkhandho, gilānupamassa viññāṇupādānakkhandhassa vatthudvārārammaṇavasena nivāsaṭṭhānato.
rows.english: 220. 4. As to simile: the materiality aggregate [as object] of clinging is like a sick-room because it is the dwelling-place, as physical basis, door, and object, of the sick man, namely, the consciousness aggregate as object of clinging.

rows.pali: Gelaññupamo vedanupādānakkhandho, ābādhakattā.
rows.english: The feeling aggregate as object of clinging is like the sickness because it afflicts.

rows.pali: Gelaññasamuṭṭhānupamo saññupādānakkhandho, kāmasaññādivasena rāgādisampayuttavedanāsabbhāvā.
rows.english: The perception aggregate as object of clinging is like the provocation of the sickness because it gives rise to feeling associated with greed, etc., owing to perception of sense desires, and so on.

rows.pali: Asappāyasevanupamo saṅkhārupādānakkhandho, vedanāgelaññassa nidānattā.
rows.english: The formations aggregate as object of clinging is like having recourse to what is unsuitable because it is the source of feeling, which is the sickness;

rows.pali: "Vedanaṃ vedanatthāya abhisaṅkharontī"ti (saṃ. ni. 3.79) hi vuttaṃ.
rows.english: for it is said: “Feeling as feeling is the formed that they form” (S III 87),

rows.pali: Tathā "akusalassa kammassa katattā upacitattā vipākaṃ kāyaviññāṇaṃ uppannaṃ hoti dukkhasahagata"nti (dha. sa. 556).
rows.english: and likewise: “Because of unprofitable kamma having been performed and stored up, resultant body-consciousness has arisen accompanied by pain” (Dhs §556).

rows.pali: Gilānupamo viññāṇupādānakkhandho, vedanāgelaññena aparimuttattā.
rows.english: The consciousness aggregate as object of clinging is like the sick man because it is never free from feeling, which is the sickness.

rows.pali: Apica cārakakāraṇaaparādhakāraṇakārakaaparādhikupamā ete bhājanabhojanabyañjanaparivesakabhuñjakūpamā cāti evaṃ upamāto vinicchayanayo viññātabbo.
rows.english: 221. Also they are (respectively) like the prison, the punishment, the offence, the punisher, and the offender. And they are like the dish, the food, the curry sauce [poured over the food], the server, and the eater.83 This is how the exposition should be known as to simile.

rows.pali: 508.Daṭṭhabbato dvidhāti saṅkhepato vitthārato cāti evaṃ dvidhā daṭṭhabbatopettha vinicchayanayo viññātabbo.
rows.english: 222. 5. Twice as to how to be seen: the exposition should be known twice as to how to be seen, namely, in brief and in detail.

rows.pali: Saṅkhepato hi pañcupādānakkhandhā āsīvisūpame (saṃ. ni. 4.238) vuttanayena ukkhittāsikapaccatthikato, bhārasuttavasena (saṃ. ni. 3.22) bhārato, khajjanīyapariyāyavasena (saṃ. ni. 3.79) khādakato, yamakasuttavasena (saṃ. ni. 3.85) aniccadukkhānattasaṅkhatavadhakato daṭṭhabbā.
rows.english: 223. In brief [that is, collectively] the five aggregates as objects of clinging should be seen as an enemy with drawn sword (S IV 174) in the Snake Simile, as a burden (S III 25) according to the Burden Sutta, as a devourer (S III 87f) according to the To-be-devoured Discourse, and as impermanent, painful, not- self, formed, and murderous, according to the Yamaka Sutta (S III 112f).

rows.pali: Vitthārato panettha pheṇapiṇḍo viya rūpaṃ daṭṭhabbaṃ, parimaddanāsahanato.
rows.english: 224. In detail [that is, individually,] matter should be regarded as a lump of froth because it will not stand squeezing,

rows.pali: Udakapubbuḷaṃ viya vedanā, muhuttaramaṇīyato.
rows.english: feeling as a bubble on water because it can only be enjoyed for an instant,

rows.pali: Marīcikā viya saññā, vippalambhanato.
rows.english: perception as a mirage because it causes illusion,

rows.pali: Kadalikkhandho viya saṅkhārā, asārakato.
rows.english: formations as a plantain trunk because they have no core,

rows.pali: Māyā viya viññāṇaṃ, vañcakato.
rows.english: and consciousness as a conjuring trick because it deceives (S III 140–42).

rows.pali: Visesato ca suḷārampi ajjhattikaṃ rūpaṃ asubhanti daṭṭhabbaṃ.
rows.english: In particular, even sublime internal materiality84 should be regarded as foul (ugly);

rows.pali: Vedanā tīhi dukkhatāhi avinimuttato dukkhāti.
rows.english: feeling should be regarded as painful because it is never free from the three kinds of suffering (see XVI.34);

rows.pali: Saññāsaṅkhārā avidheyyato anattāti.
rows.english: perception and formations as not-self because they are unmanageable;

rows.pali: Viññāṇaṃ udayabbayadhammato aniccanti daṭṭhabbaṃ.
rows.english: and consciousness as impermanent because it has the nature of rise and fall.

rows.pali: 509.Evaṃ passantassatthasiddhitoti evañca saṅkhepavitthāravasena dvidhā passato yā atthasiddhi hoti, tatopi vinicchayanayo viññātabbo.
rows.english: 225. 6. As to good for one seeing thus: good comes to be accomplished in one who sees in the two ways thus in brief and in detail. And the way of definition should be known according to that,

rows.pali: Seyyathidaṃ – saṅkhepato tāva pañcupādānakkhandhe ukkhittāsikapaccatthikādibhāvena passanto khandhehi na vihaññati.
rows.english: that is to say, firstly, one who sees the five aggregates as objects of clinging in the form of an enemy with drawn sword, etc., is not worried by the aggregates, but one who sees materiality, etc.,

rows.pali: Vitthārato pana rūpādīni pheṇapiṇḍādisadisabhāvena passanto na asāresu sāradassī hoti.
rows.english: in detail as a lump of froth, etc., is not one who sees a core in the coreless.

rows.pali: Visesato ca ajjhattikarūpaṃ asubhato passanto kabaḷīkārāhāraṃ parijānāti, asubhe subhanti vipallāsaṃ pajahati.
rows.english: 226. And in particular, one who sees internal materiality as foul (ugly) fully understands nutriment consisting of physical nutriment.

rows.pali: Kāmoghaṃ uttarati, kāmayogena visaṃyujjati, kāmāsavena anāsavo hoti, abhijjhākāyaganthaṃ bhindati, kāmupādānaṃ na upādiyati.
rows.english: He abandons the perversion [of perceiving] beauty in the foul (ugly), he crosses the flood of sense desire, he is loosed from the bond of sense desire, he becomes canker-free as regards the canker of sense desire, he breaks the bodily tie of covetousness. He does not cling with sense-desire clinging.

rows.pali: Vedanaṃdukkhato passanto phassāhāraṃ parijānāti, dukkhe sukhanti vipallāsaṃ pajahati, bhavoghaṃ uttarati, bhavayogena visaṃyujjati, bhavāsavena anāsavo hoti, byāpādakāyaganthaṃ bhindati, sīlabbatupādānaṃ na upādiyati.
rows.english: 227.One who sees feeling as pain fully understands nutriment consisting of contact. He abandons the perversion of perceiving pleasure in the painful. He crosses the flood of becoming. He is loosed from the bond of becoming. He becomes canker-free as regards the canker of becoming. He breaks the bodily tie of ill will. He does not cling with rules-and-vows clinging.

rows.pali: Saññaṃ saṅkhāre ca anattato passanto manosañcetanāhāraṃ parijānāti, anattani attāti vipallāsaṃ pajahati, diṭṭhoghaṃ uttarati, diṭṭhiyogena visaṃyujjati, diṭṭhāsavena anāsavo hoti.
rows.english: 228. One who sees perception and formations as not-self fully understands nutriment consisting of mental volition. He abandons the perversion of perceiving self in the not-self. He crosses the flood of views. He is loosed from the bond of views.

rows.pali: Idaṃsaccābhinivesakāyaganthaṃ bhindati, attavādupādānaṃ na upādiyati.
rows.english: He breaks the bodily tie of interpretations (insistence) that “This is the truth.” He does not cling with self-theory clinging.

rows.pali: Viññāṇaṃ aniccato passanto viññāṇāhāraṃ parijānāti, anicce niccanti vipallāsaṃ pajahati, avijjoghaṃ uttarati, avijjāyogena visaṃyujjati, avijjāsavena anāsavo hoti, sīlabbataparāmāsakāyaganthaṃ bhindati, diṭṭhupādānaṃ na upādiyati.
rows.english: 229. One who sees consciousness as impermanent fully understands nutriment consisting of consciousness. He abandons the perversion of perceiving permanence in the impermanent. He crosses the flood of ignorance. He is loosed from the bond of ignorance. He becomes canker-free as regards the canker of ignorance. He breaks the bodily tie of holding to rules and vows. He does not [cling with false-] view clinging.
